text: <par>The first syllable of this form means 'eye' (< PTB <reconstruction>*s-mik ⪤ *s-myak</reconstruction>), and the third syllable means 'hair' (< PTB <reconstruction>*mul ⪤ *mil</reconstruction>).</par>
<par>For alternative etymologies, see STC notes 102, 109, 137. The Jingpho word for ‘hand’ (<latinform>lətáʔ</latinform> in the STC’ s notation) may be monomorphemically from *lak or *glak.</par>
<par>or 1390/1390? Another reason to suspect ts/tsh is from another Etym?</par>
<par>#1390 is glossed LIVER / HEART / BILE / BITTER. If 'heart' and 'liver' are one semantic area in this region, this Jingpho form might be a loan from Chinese xinzang 'heart'. </par>
<par>Borrowing from Chinese, cf. Chinese 洋芋	yángyù 'potato'.</par>
<par>Loan from Chinese, cf. Chinese 工人 gōngrén 'worker'.</par>
<par>Loan from Chinese, cf. Chinese yínháng 銀行 'bank (for depositing money)'.</par>
<par>Loan from Chinese, cf. Chinese bànfǎ 辦法 'means, method'.</par>
<par>The Jingpho final is irregular: one would expect <latinform>-n</latinform> as the reflex of <latinform>*-l</latinform>.</par>
<par>Loan from Chinese, cf. Chinese guònián 过年.</par>
